div.head: Soviet Power Collapse in Eastern Europe--Strategic Arms Control (October 1989-April 1990)Source: George H.W. Bush Library, Bush Presidential Records, Brent Scowcroft Collection, USSR Chronological Files, START Files, OA/ID 91122-003, Soviet Power Collapse in Eastern Europe-Strategic Arms Control (October 1989-April 1990). Secret. A stamped notation indicates that Scowcroft saw the paper. A stamped notation indicates that Bush saw the paper. Bush wrote at the top of the paper: "1st Week in March." He also wrote in the right-corner: "Patty (1) save in personal files (2) Major Dramatic proposal-I told [illegible] go ahead GB 3-15."
div.p: SECRET RECORD ID: 8908097 NSC/S PROFILE RECEIVED: 10 OCT 89 20 TO: SCOWCROFT PHPEN FILE FROM: TOBEY - DOC DATE: 06 OCT 89 KANTER SOURCE REF: KEYWORDS: DEFENSE POLICY SPACE POLICY ARMS CONTROL START USSR LIMITED ACCESS PERSONS: SUBJECT: US DEFENSE & SPACE REVIEW ACTION: OBE PER SCOWCROFT DUE DATE: 14 OCT 89 STATUS: C STAFF OFFICER: TOBEY LOGREF: FILES: PA NSCIF: CODES: DOCUMENT DISTRIBUTION FOR ACTION FOR CONCURRENCE FOR INFO KANTER TOBEY White House Guidelines E.O. 12958, SEC 3.4 (B) September 11, 2006 By Cop NARA, Date 3}i7feq COMMENTS: a DISPATCHED BY DATE BY HAND W/ATTCH OPENED BY: NSAJC CLOSED BY: NSJRP Doc 10F 1 a #e=ea4##ee§e.A..........

a ---- E te rs] ni -- wis doi i ¢ NATIONAL SECURITY COUNCIL LIMITED ACCESS HES WASHINGTON, D.C. 20506 8097 _ October 6, 1989. a ACTION i a MEMORANDUM FOR BRENT SCOWCROFT THROUGH: ARNOLD KANTER G2 FROM: wrcarafossy SUBJECT: U.S. Defense & Space Review The time has come for a serious review of our Defense & Space position for three reasons: (1) during the broader review of national strategy last Spring, agencies were not prepared to step up to the issues; (2) the Soviet position on linkage presented at the Wyoming Ministerial has brought Defense & Space issues to center stage, and, (3) Ambassadors Cooper and Burt have sent backchannel messages to Secretaries Cheney and Baker respectively suggesting ways to modify our position. Our policy options are sufficiently detailed that they require staffing before they can usefully be engaged by senior policy makers. The paper at Tab I is an example of what such an options paper might look like. But to conduct a successful review, we need to have top-down guidance from your colleagues to their staffs to take the review seriously. The top-down guidance can be obtained in two ways. You might have an informal discussion with Secretaries Baker and Cheney, Judge Webster and General Powell that simply concentrates on the importance of the review and the need to make it a thorough and candid discussion of options. Or, you might use the paper at Tab II, to focus such a discussion and provide more specific guidance to the bureaucracy as to what is and is not under consideration. This latter approach might be particularly useful should the President wish to see options for big proposals (e.g. dropping heretofore U.S. insistence on a right to deploy SDI, perhaps in exchange for a ban on MIRV'ed ICBMs or some other large objective.) The advantage of the paper at Tab II is that it could serve as the basis for more concrete guidance and therefore a more productive review. The disadvantage is that it may not be possible to provide such guidance. Regardless of your decision on the paper at Tab II, we also need commitments for your colleagues that the review will be handled SEGREP DECLASGFY ON: OADR DECLASSIFIED - - PER E.O. 13526 fe Mua 12/2/2016

TN Ee SOT ahi SEEREP quietly. Leaks will hurt us badly, in Geneva and on the Hill. In order to minimize the possibility of leaks we recommend that you ask your colleagues to designate one official per agency to participate in a compartmented, NSC-staff chaired review, at the deputy assistant secretary level. Papers may be shared up, but not down. RECOMMENDATIONS That you approve our conducting a review of the U.S. Defense & Space position as outlined above. Approve Disapprove That you discuss with your colleagues the importance of the review. Approve Disapprove Or, discuss with your colleagues the paper at Tab II. Approve Disapprove Tab I DST 003 Tab II Sensitive Arms Control document SEEREP ty

-seense SEGREF October 10, 1989 = DST 03 (U) Purpose. To review U.S. Defense & Space positions in light of developments at the Wyoming ministerial meeting. Ue) U.S. Position. In returning to the Defense & Space talks in June, the U.S. negotiating group was instructed to pursue the following objectives: o to preserve the right to pursue the SDI program within the obligations of the ABM Treaty in order to determine the technical feasibility of advanced strategic defenses; o to discuss with the Soviet Union a cooperative transition to a strategic regime based on effective defenses; and, o to make explicit the right to deploy effective strategic ballistic missile defenses after a non-withdrawal period. (&) The means by which the negotiating group was to pursue these objectives were several fold: o to preserve U.S. rights to conduct SDI testing, while countering Soviet arguments that permitted and prohibited activities under the ABM Treaty must be agreed, the U.S. offered a unilateral assurance that we would only conduct ABM tests from designated test satellites, and that the number of these platforms would be no greater than fifteen. Furthermore, the U.S. proposed that each side undertake to notify the other of launches of ABM test satellites and ABM tests in . orbit. (#4 o to promote stability during a transition, the U.S. offered a protocol to a Defense & Space Treaty, including reciprocal briefings and visits to strategic defense sites. We also proposed on-going discussions on strategic stability to further a stable transition. (34 o to make explicit a right to deploy strategic defenses after a non-withdrawal period, the U.S. draft Defense & Space Treaty built on the Washington Summit Joint Statement, in which Gorbachev agreed that after a non-withdrawal period, each side would be free to pursue its own course of action. (8%) Se icy on: one =O ECGREF PER E.0O. 13526 1010-28 06-ug ma ith}rolb

seuss SEGREF The U.S. Defense & Space negotiating group returned to Geneva for Round XII instructed to: probe the Soviets for details of their new approach on Defense & Space issues; continue discussions on predictability measures; and, seek to determine if the Soviets have new proposals on activities under the ABM Treaty. If asked about whether the U.S. Treaty remains on the table, the negotiating group was instructed to say that it is. Moreover, the U.S. team was instructed to express the continued U.S. commitment to evaluate the feasibility of effective strategic defenses, and to deploy them when ready and to oppose linkage or preconditions to START in any form. Finally, the U.S. negotiating group was authorized to draw on Secretary Baker's talking points from the ministerial meeting, and did so, making the points that the U.S. seeks a separate Defense & Space Treaty, embodying a commitment to discussions of strategic stability lasting not more than three years, at the end of which both sides would be free to decide their course of action on strategic defenses. (8) Soviet Position. In June, the Soviets returned to the Defense & Space talks with little changed. They continued to link completion of a START Treaty to a U.S. commitment to adhere to a narrow definition of the ABM Treaty. They sought to use the U.S.-proposed predictability measures as a means of verifying compliance with this standard. And, they continued to refuse to work a joint draft Defense & Space Treaty although such work had been agreed to at the Washington Summit and subsequent ministerial meetings. One new development was their hint that they would seek to discuss ASAT limitations in Round XII. (8) At the Wyoming ministerial, the Soviets announced that they were dropping their requirement for a U.S. commitment not to withdraw from the ABM Treaty as a pre-condition for START. Both sides characterized this as delinkage. The Soviets made clear however, that they claim a right to withdraw from a START Treaty if they determine that the U.S. has gone beyond the ABM Treaty as they define it. V4 Thus, the Soviets have moved from linking their D&S - position to the paper of a START Treaty to the reductions following a START Treaty. (8) The Soviets also said that they would have proposals for resolving permitted and prohibited activities under the ABM Treaty in a protocol to the ABM Treaty, which would also include predictability measures. They have hinted that the U.S. would find these proposals more acceptable than the 1987 "list" proposal. Most recently they proposed that the sides sign up to the narrow interpretation of the ABM Treaty, citing the "Brown Statement" on the distinction between research and development. (83) seenes SEGRET

. fw 57 oe SEGRE Thus, in dropping their demand for a non-withdrawal commitment, -the Soviets have attempted to drop the Washington Summit Joint Statement framework for a Defense & Space agreement, and in particular the Gorbachev-agreed notion of a right to defenses a specified period of time. Below are options for dealing with this new Soviet approach. (#9 Activities Under the ABM Treaty. Option 1. Current U.S. Position. To clarify testing permitted during the non-withdrawal period and to address stated Soviet concerns, the U.S. provided to the Soviets on October 28, 1988 an authoritative unilateral assurance that: "In order to demonstrate that the testing of space-based components capable of substituting for ABM interceptor missiles, which is permitted by the ABM Treaty, does not represent the deployment of such components, the United States is prepared to carry out such permitted testing only from designated ABM test satellites. Any space object in earth orbit from which a component of an ABM system capable of substituting for an ABM interceptor missile or its elements in flight trajectory shall be designated an ABM test satellite. Further, it is the view of the United States that the number of U.S. designated ABM test satellites in orbit simultaneously shall not exceed a number well short of that associated with any realistic deployed capability. The United States believes the number 15 falls well below that threshold." (# To help clarify ABM testing permitted during the non-withdrawal period and to provide for greater predictability, the U.S. also proposed in the draft Predictability Protocol that the Parties shall provide each other with the following data: 1. Identification, no more than 12 hours after launch of each space object in earth orbit from which a component of an ABM system capable of substituting for an ABM interceptor missile is to be tested to counter a strategic ballistic missile or its elements in flight trajectory; 2. Notification within 12 hours after a re-entry of an ABM Test Satellite from its orbit or after a change in orbit; and, 3. Notification of a test using such a space object in earth orbit to counter a strategic ballistic missile or its elements in flight trajectory no less than SECREP -3- way awe

Seer +: This option is supported by: (2) Option 2.7 Agree to Negotiate Permitted and Prohibited Activities Under the ABM Treaty. (8) Under this approach, the United States would indicate a willingness to negotiate with the Soviets a list of permitted and prohibited activities with respect to testing in space. The list of activities would be based on a framework of technical characteristics of the devices to be tested. For example, lasers tested in space might be limited below a certain threshold of brightness, or kinetic interceptors tested in space might be limited below a certain acceleration or velocity. Tests below the thresholds would be permitted in any mode, including ABM and ASAT. Tests above the thresholds would be prohibited. 7-4) Those who support this option believe: 1. Congress will demand clarification of the ABM Treaty as a precondition to START ratification. Senator Nunn has already laid this marker down. Moreover, he can expect support from conservatives on the Hill who will seek to ensure that SDI testing rights are well. understood. 2. The Soviets will not accept the current U.S. proposal in any form. The Soviets have refused for over a year to negotiate on the basis of the January 1988 U.S. é proposals. There is no reason to believe they will alter their position. 3. Congress will never permit the U.S. to test under the broad interpretation. Congress has restricted SDI funds to testing under the narrow interpretation for three years in a row and by substantial margins in the House. There is no reason to believe Congress will alter its position. 4. Negotiating permitted and prohibited activities with the Soviet Union may enable us to test under some sort of "narrow plus" formulation. For example, space-based kinetic interceptors might be tested against a strategic missile in ballistic trajectory so long as the interceptor's acceleration capability fell below a given threshold. Such a test would not be permitted under the U.S. understanding of the narrow interpretation. (8) Option 3. Propose Clarifying Permitted ABM Treaty Activities in Some Other Fashion. (8) To be provided by agencies with different ideas, if any. . SSCRER -5-£2_ a am) -a

a g S SECREL Right to Deploy. The U.S. draft Defense & Space Treaty calls for an explicit right to deploy defenses after the non-withdrawal period. The rationale for this position was two-fold: first, it was a quid pro quo for the Soviet demand of a non-withdrawal period; second, it furthered the U.S. objective of facilitating deployment of strategic defenses should they prove feasible. The position was crafted to diminish political difficulties that would be inherent in withdrawing from the ABM Treaty. (8) The Soviet move at Wyoming probably reflects their judgment that they overestimated our ability to withdraw from the ABM Treaty. They were willing to drop their demand for a non-withdrawal commitment confident that they still explicitly held hostage START reductions to U.S. adherence to the ABM Treaty as they define it. They were particularly willing to make this move inasmuch as it enabled them to evade Gorbachev's agreement at the Washington Summit that after a non-withdrawal period "both sides would be free to pursue their own course of action" with respect to strategic defenses. At the time, Secretary Carlucci confirmed with Shevardnadze that this meant deployment. The U.S. can respond to the Soviet move at Wyoming in several ways. Option 1. Modify the Current Position. (8) The present U.S. draft treaty provides that after the non-withdrawal period, either side would have the right to deploy defenses upon six months notice of intent to deploy. The Soviet move at Wyoming removed the hook to hang the right to deploy on. But the U.S. draft could be recrafted to maintain that right in several ways. (8) Sub-option A. A right to deploy tied to stability talks. 2) Under this option, the U.S. would propose that should either side decide in the future to move to deployment beyond the ABM Treaty, that side must provide a plan to the other on how such deployment could proceed in a stable way. This plan would form the basis for the future discussions on strategic stability that were suggested in Gorbachev's recent letter. The discussions would take place for a period of no longer than two years, after which, unless the sides had agreed otherwise, either side would be free to deploy defenses after giving six months notice. (% Those who support this sub-option believe that: ---- Ale., view |b

hearings. (8) Those who sUpport this option believe that: 1. The Soviets will never concede a U.S. right to deploy defenses under any circumstances and the U.S. right to withdraw from the ABM Treaty is sufficient to protect our interests. 2. It would be very difficult politically for the Soviets to reverse START reductions and at least the onus would be on the Soviets. In this regard, the future discussions on strategic stability could further support for the U.S. position. 3. There are not enough votes in the Senate to block a START Treaty simply because the Soviets link reductions to abandonment of SDI deployments in a unilateral statement. Those who might do so can simply support a stated U.S. view disavowing the Soviet unilateral statement. ws) This option is supported by: 1s) Option 3. Rely on ABM Treaty Withdrawal Rights and Do Not Counter Soviet Unilateral Statements on Linkage. (8) Under this option, the U.S. would rely on its right to withdraw from the ABM Treaty if it wished to deploy strategic defenses. We would state that we recognize the interrelationship between offensive and defensive forces. We would agree to discuss this interrelationship in the future discussions on strategic stability. (8s) Those who support this option believe that: 1. The Soviets would not concede a right to deploy defenses under any circumstances. 2. We cannot take a START Treaty to the Senate with conflicting U.S. and Soviet views on linkage to START reductions. 3. As a practical matter, reductions should be linked to the future of strategic defense deployments. The U.S. would certainly wish to review whether or not to pursue START reductions in the face of additional Soviet strategic defenses and we should, therefore, understand the Soviet concerns. (8J This option is supported by: (8) SBEREP 8a eee ¢ yi i A t VALI bee

a ie ae SEoRE t Issue Should the U.S. modify its current positions on (a) its rights to develop and teSt SDI under the ABM Treaty, and/or (b) its right to deploy SDI? Background The Soviets say they have delinked START and Defense and Space in order to facilitate early agreement on START. At the same time, they have made clear that they reserve the right to withdraw from the START Treaty if the U.S. goes outside the boundaries of the ABM Treaty as the Soviets define those boundaries. These moves have occurred against a backdrop of continuing erosion of Congressional budgetary support for SDI. They also have spawned public reactions from Sen. Nunn and Congressman Aspin (privately echoed by Sen. Lugar) that a START agreement which is based on a U.S.-Soviet "agreement to disagree" about the terms of the ABM Treaty would get a cold reception on the Hill. The key U.S. objectives in the Defense and Space Talks have been: ds Broad interpretation of the ABM Treaty. To secure Soviet acknowledgement of (or acquiescence in) our right to conduct SDI research, development, and testing under the broad interpretation of the ABM Treaty; and 2. Right to deploy. To be able to deploy SDI without the need to abrogate the ABM Treaty and to secure Soviet agreement to (or acquiescence in) that course of action. In the view of some, the shift in the Soviet position provides either the occasion or requirement for the U.S. to review one or both of these objectives and/or our strategies for achieving them. Such a review will require top-down direction to be effective. The decision on how to proceed with be affected in part by judgments about the prospects for realizing our two objectives, and by the likely impact of the alternatives on SDI, on the resolution of outstanding issues in START, and on Congressional support for SDI and strategic force modernization. Discussion Visible and concrete progress on START could help sustain Congressional support for both offensive force modernization and SDI (the latter by demonstrating that SDI is not an "obstacle" to a START agreement). Depending on whatever is agreed with the Soviets about terms of the ABM Treaty, the result could provide more latitude for SDI testing than Congress might permit in the absence of such an agreement. Conversely, progress on SDI could aes PER E.0. 13526 C2 eae Lo1o-27g0b-mk me ifr /rolb

facet oe ' Sinner Soo t be hampered, either by the imposition of new limits on SDIrelated activities, or by the erosion of political and budgetary support for the program. Moreover, if progress on START is seen to have come at the expense of SDI, SDI supporters may be unwilling to work for START ratification. We can respond either tactically or strategically to the Soviet moves. Tactical questions include whether to engage the Soviets on their proposal to discuss "clarifications" of the ABM Treaty that would define what SDI testing would be allowed and forbidden, whether to continue to press the Soviets to acknowledge a U.S. right to deploy SDI after an agreed period, and how to engage the Soviet proposal for a mutual understanding about ABM developments that would allow a side to withdraw from the START Treaty. In principle, we could engage the Soviets on issues such as these for some time whether or not we modify our two core objectives, provided U.S.-Soviet agreement on them was not deemed essential to our goals for START or SDI. The strategic questions turn on whether and, if so, when we should modify either or both of our D&S objectives. Specifically, should we: se Be prepared to seek agreement with the Soviets on "clarifications" of the ABM Treaty that would permit some SDI activities that go beyond the "narrow" interpretation, but would prohibit some SDI activities that would be accommodated under the "broad" interpretation; or should we continue to press for Soviet acceptance of our rights under the "broad" interpretation? 2. Be prepared to defer resolution of our right to deploy SDI after an agreed period; or should we continue to press for Soviet acceptance of our right to deploy without either requiring us to abrogate the ABM Treaty or agreeing to Soviet abrogation of the START Treaty; or should we be willing to put the possibility of delaying SDI deployment "on the table" as a trade for a Major Soviet concession in START (e.g., agreement to a deMIRVing initiative). Decisions on whether to address either or both strategic questions now rest essentially on political judgments that must be made at senior levels. Following these decisions, the interagency process can develop the appropriate options and analysis for senior level review. Hie dil oe ee

Chairman Gorbachev of the USSR - October 18, sea" yw \ Chairman Gorbachev apprises you of the positive impression he has of your meeting with Foreign Minister Shevardnadze. He discusses matters on arms control, chemical weapons, verification and bilateral relations. The cable merits your personal review. Prime Minister Martens of Belgium - October 14, 1989 Prime Minister Martens acknowledges your letter of August 22nd on Panama. He states that Belgium, in coordination with the EEC, ve supports both the Opposition's efforts towards achieving democracy and the actions taken by the OAS against the Noriega regime. Prime Minister Lubbers of The Netherlands - October 5, 1989 piins Minister Lubbers, in cooperation with the EPC, offers is full support to the government of Colombia in its campaign against drug traffickers. , 6901 ea A, sty PRES NOTED SUMMARY LE PREMIER MINISTRE A4/CS/1045/89 Bruxelles, le 14 septembre 1989 SCaag BO25428 "89 OCT-27 ii PN 7 Mh a pnt updo, i; J'ai l'honneur de me référer a votre lettre du 22 aot 1989 relative a la situation au Panama. Les arguments que vous y avez développés ont retenu toute mon attention. Dans cette optique, les Autorités de mon pays ont participé activement aux consultations des douze Etats membres de la C.E.E. au titre de la coopération politique européenne. Les instances européennes sont, dans ce cadre, convenues de ce qui suit: = suspension de la représentation des Douze a Panama aux cérémonies officielles et aux invitations par des membres du Gouvernement ou des Forces de défense; - intensification des relations avec 1'opposition; - refus a l'avenir de tout engagement de soutenir le Panama dans les organisations internationales; 7 ralentissement des programmes bilatéraux de coopération technique a l'exception de l'aide 4 caractére humanitaire ou social; Son Excellence Monsieur George BUSH Président des Etats-Unis d'Amérique WASHINGTON

2 = maintien de la consultation sur la situation au Panama avec 1'OEA et les pays du mécanisme permanent (Groupe des Huit); = examen par la Commission Européenne de la suspension de la participation du Panama a la coopération économique dans le cadre du dialogue permanent CEE-Amérique Centrale. La Présidence de la CEE, au nom des douze Etats membres, va entreprendre une démarche auprés de 1'0.E.A. pour informer cette organisation de la portée des sanctions et mesures prises a l'égard du régime que le Général NORIEGA impose au Panama. La Belgique ne manquera certes pas, a l'avenir, d'adopter toujours dans le cadre de la coopération politique des Douze, toute autre initiative susceptible de traduire la solidarité des Nations démocratiques avec le peuple panaméen dans son aspiration a la démocratie et dans sa volonté d'assurer le respect des Droits de 1'Homme. Je vous prie d'agréer, Monsieur le Président, 1'assurance de ma trés haute considération \ Sf? Gu MARTENS, Premier Ministre

' 8689 pe on PRES NOTED SUMMARY MINISTER-PRESIDENT GBaAc4iTT 8925137 a9 QO7-28 9208 AR The Hague, 5 October 1989 a. MWe. \ Te ident, dear George The Netherlands too is concerned about' the situation in Colombia, which demonstrates that resolute action against the drug traffickers is urgently needed. The government of Colombia can count on the support of the Netherlands and that of its partners in European Political Cooperation in its campaign against the drug traffickers. This point was made most recently in the EPC-statement of 26 September 1989. Minister Hans van den Broek is devoting a great deal of attention to this issue and, in consultation with his EPC partners, is considering specific ways in which assistance may be provided. He will continue to keep in close touch with Jim Baker on this matter. R.F.M. Lubbers The President of the United States of America The White House WASHINGTON DC

CONEERENTTAT W/SEERES ATTACHMENTS NATIONAL SECURITY COUNCIL WASHINGTON, D.C. 20506 ; 8700 November 1, 1989 r Deputy Nati Seo ain has seen, ACTION MEMORANDUM FOR BRENT SCOWCROFT THROUGH: G. RECOMMENDATION That you forward the attached summary (Tab I) to the President. Many, pw Gorbachev has so regularly done the unexpected that some sort of surprise has almost come to seem the norm. Many of Gorbachevs unexpected moves have stemmed from the urgency with which he is pursuing domestic change and his drive to manage rather than respond to events. He has demonstrated that he sees his foreign policy and domestic goals to be inextricably intertwined, and has set an ambitious foreign policy agenda for next year. At the same time he faces a deteriorating situation at home and unprecedented turmoil next door in Eastern Europe. while paradoxically enjoying his strangest position ever within the Soviet leadership. These circumstances suggest he will take more bold initiatives sooner rather than Jater.!) The most likely areas for bold, unexpected initiatives are strategic and naval arms control: o On START, a variety of specific initiatives to accelerate progress, or a package of far-reaching strategic cuts to replace the current START framework. o On naval issues, proposals for unilateral or mutual reductions--in forces or This paper was prepared by the Office of Soviet Analysis' ee | ____.. Praise and compliments can be directed to any or all of the above. _ DECLASSIFIED IN PART 1 PER E.0. 13526 2.00%~0984-MR -Seeret!? Me [2/2016 --

naval presence--designed to break down US resistance to holding talks on naval arms control, The dynamics of a relatively unstructured meeting, however, give Gorbachev the opportunity to offer initiatives affecting any aspect of US-Soviet relations. [) 2 ~Seeret

: Secret [2° Scope Note WARNING--The following essay is the result of a deliberate attempt to flesh out less likely or unlikely actions and statements, especially those that would require a US response, The principal criterion for inclusion was the ability to make a plausible case in favor of some event. We did not systematically attempt to contrast these less likely excursions with what we judge to be most likely, nor did we generally present the reasons specific initiatives would not be taken. ee 3

Setting the Stage: Changes in Style and Tactics In his December meeting with the President, Gorbachev might deliberately abandon his normally confident and persuasive demeanor in favor of a less confident or a more confrontational stance. The main cause would be the deteriorating Soviet internal situation and his urgent need for rapid progress on foreign policy issues that he sees as directly affecting his domestic agenda. Gorbachev hopes to frame next fall's Party Congress with concrete successes, especially in arms control. PUB Any new tack could surface before rather than during the meeting with the President. In the runup to the meeting, particularly while Gorbachev is in Italy, he could make a series of public statements that would be intended to raise expectations and to set the agenda for the talks. He might portray the meeting as a full "summit," hoping that the United States would feel greater pressure to conclude some significant agreemer: it the meeting, or at least to agree To Joint TNGUaGE INS "communique that laid out the goals for future negotiations and put some time limits on reaching some of the most significant agreements. ese Gorbachev as Supplicant. The Soviet leader could come to the December meeting with his hat in his hand. He would stress his own beleagured political position and urge the President to take more concrete economic and political steps to support perestroyka. Increased US support, he would argue, would support the position of reformers within the USSR and lessen the possibility that internal dissent would reach the point of requiring sustained repressive measures. Gorbachev would also ask for the President's understanding in the event of a crackdown on ethnic or labor unrest, and if possible an agreement to temper US criticism. ee 4 Secret fs

Gorbachev as "Tough Guy". In this scenario, Gorbachev would inform the President that in the coming months the government might have to use force to contain disorder and that it was necessary for the United States to understand the need for this. He would warn that it would be counterproductive for the United States to attempt to dictate Soviet "goad behavior" by holding up progress on arms control and other issues. He would justify this stance by informing the President that some groups in the Soviet Union were threatening violence and attempting to break up the state, and remind him that the United States had fought a civil war in order to preserve its Union. eer ooo TS AEE me ee BHI SB ml oN NER Gorbachev might also adopt a confrontational "put up or shut up" approach, trying to put the President on the defensive by bringing up Soviet arms control and compliance concerns. He would highlight specific examples of Soviet willingness to address US concerns, and argue that the United States had not demonstrated good faith in responding appropriately. For example, he might argue forcefully that the current high levels of emigration permitted by the Soviet Union justified not only a waiver of the Jackson-Vanick amendment and the granting of most favored nation trading status to th Soviet Union, but also that some more significant trade or economic agreements shoul be concluded that would help the Soviet Union out of its economic morass. Other examples could include the decision to take down the Krasnoyarsk radar and the unilateral decision to reduce Soviet tank advantages in Europe. He might argue that the lack of proportionate US response was making his domestic position more precarious and undercutting the consensus in the leadership for further progress on arms control eee We have not ruled out "negative" surprises, but we judge any surprises are more likely to take the form of concessions or proposals to further arms control and positive bilateral relations. In most of the following sections, therefore, have begun with 'positive' surprises. Re 5 Seerer

Seerer : EEO SIAC A AWTS Arms Control Surprises are most likely in the areas of strategic arms, where several remaining "big issues" can only be resolved at a high level; and, given the meeting's at-sea setting and recent Soviet emphasis, in naval arms control. Gorbachev could introduce a variety of Spocinic initiatives either to accelerate progress on START and CFE or in the context of a challenge to the United States to begin moving to the 'next stage' of arms control. Moscow envisions this 'next stage' as including START II, CFE II, SNF, naval arms control, and negotiations on a complete ban of nuclear tests. Strategic Arms. Gorbachev is trying to set up next year's spring Washington summit as the venue for signing a START agreement or at least resolving all major issues. |f Moscow believes the United States would be receptive to further concessions and proposals to meet this timetable, Gorbachev might make proposals to solve the remaining major stumblingblocks: o Dropping the Wyoming ministerial call for an agreed US~Soviet statement on the right to withdraw frapr START if the ABM Treaty is violated, relying on the US Congress to prevent US violations of the ABM Treaty; v p Accepting US proposals for non-binding limits on SLCMs, perhap: in return for a commitment to continue discussions on SLCMs in START II or naval arms control talks; o Agree to US proposed counting rules for nuclear ALCMs, perhaps in exchange for US concessions on the lesser issue of ALCM range. (°" If Gorbachev judges the US will not move on START without a dramatic push, he could go further and put forward one or more of the following unilateral initiatives or proposals for mutual reductions. Any such initiatives would have the added aim of undermining public and Congressional support for US strategic programs. In rough order 6 5 1}

of increasing impact, Soviet initiatives could include: © Unilaterally accelerating cuts in older ballistic missiles (SS-11s, SS-17s, SS-N-6, etc.) This would be relatively easy to do given cuts already underway and would provide some propaganda benefits, though its military impact would be minimized by ongoing ICBM modernization programs. o Offer to cap Blackjack deployments--which we think have slowed in any case--either unilaterally or in return for limits on the B-2. o Announce a unilateral 50 percent or more reduction in SS-18s without waiting for START (probably by dismantling the SS-18 mod 4s that would have to be eliminated anyway under START and modernizing the remaining 154 heavy ICBMs allowed). Moscow would expect this to have a major impact on public opinion, and would be consistent with Soviet plans for the heavy ICBM force under START. The Soviets have already altered the SS-18 mod-5 conversion program to accomodate future START limits. Moreover, the mod-5's areater accuracy will enable the Soviets to meet their targeting requirements with fewer heavy missiles. Other strategic programs--the silo-based version ot the SS-24, and the Blackjack heavy bomber--have also been unilaterally curtailed and Gorbachev could include them in such a declaration. This would be uesigned to underline the Soviet commitment to START and put pressure on the Unied States to cut back --" such as the B-2, Trident, SDI, and Peacekeeper © Offer to_mutually eliminate all MIRVed mobile missiles, i.e. the railmobile versions of the SS-24 and Peacekeeper. By trading an existing program for one the US has not yet implemented the Soviets would gain credit for going the extra mile for arms control. They would still have one survivable mobile ICBM, the single-RV SS~25. 7. . 5 0)(3)

o Offer to phase-out all heavy ICBMs as part of START, perhaps in return for US concessions on Peacekeeper, cruise missiles, Stealth aircraft, or other systems of high concern. Be Alternatively, Gorbachev might package a number of the more far-reaching proposals and present them as an alternative to the present START framework. For example, he could propose an immediate cut to 3000 nuclear warheads to include a ban on heavy ICBMs and SLCMs--~all ideas that have been publicly discussed by Soviet arms control experts. This might be done if Gorbachev judges START as presently constituted does not go far enough and threatens to drag out beyond the time when Soviet decisions have to be made on proceeding with key strategic programs. Pe. lf Gorbachev prefers not to disrupt the present START framework he may choose to Present some or all of the above proposals as part of the Soviet annreach to the next phase of strategic arms control, START !I Soviet negotiators have already suggested that the two sides begin talks on strategic stability immediately after sie «onclusion of START. By outlining the Soviet vision of deeper cuts in the future, perhans:n the context of the eventual elimination of all nuclear weapons, Gorbachev would inersas2 pressure for US flexibility now to finish START |. He could propose that the two sies agree to continue negotiations on key issues such as SLCM verification and limits un SOI testing, hoping this would allow the two sides to quickly conclude START while keening key issues on the bilateral agenda. ' Gorbachev could suggest ending the Defense and Space talks in light of Moscow's decision to drop linkage between START and a DST treaty. He could claim that remaining issues should be dealt with in the context of Soviet proposals for a protocol to the ABM Treaty regarding permitted and prohibited activities and call for a special ABM Treaty Review session. | 8 seeret{"™

An even more radical step would be to cali for a mutua! ban on Soviet BMD and US SDI activities, including dismantlement of the Moscow ABM system, as a way of increasing strategic stability and predictability. This could be coupled with an on-site inspection regime for laboratories engaged in research on strategic defenses. There is a chance Gorbachev could announce a unilateral decision to dismantie the Moscow ABM system, which has been criticized for its high cost and limited utility against modern ballistic missiles. This would be designed to put pressure on US funding for SDI. Pe Although Gorbachev is likely to emphasize Soviet concessions and further mutual cuts in arms. he may seek to balance his presentation by criticizing specific US positions. For instance, in the wake of the decision to dismantle the Krasnoyarsk radar and admit it was a violation of the ABM Treaty, Gorbachev could press for greater US action to "address Soviet concerns" on US radars in Greenland and the UK Such a move might be designed to help build European pressure on the United States to eliminate these two radars or at least to prevent construction at Fylingdales in the United Kingdom. He could privately complain that he needs some positive US response to lis controversial decisions to allay pressure from domestic critics and hint that che lack of such signals could complicate working out the dismantlement procedures 'or Krasnoyarsk. Paes Gorbachev could press the President on US plans to sal! Trident missiles to Great Britain and insist that this would leave a loophole for circumventing a START treaty. He might suggest that this issue be addressed in START lI, perhaps by including Great Sritain in the negotiations. Rees Naval. The Soviets have been mounting a full court press on naval arms control in the wake of Gorbachev's Finland visit, and the December meeting's Mediterranean venue provides an occasion for a range of regional and bilateral initiatives. The major Soviet aim at this point is to break down US resistance to holding discussions and to make sure 9 Seeret

naval forces are on the agenda for future talks. Gorbachev might also calculate that Soviet initiatives could complicate US negotiations for naval basing rights with Greece. the Philippines and other countries. Specific initiatives could include: o Proposing mutual withdrawals of fleet components from the Mediterranean, e.g. nuclear-powered or armed warships, aircraft carriers, submarines or SLCM platforms. Alternatively, he could make a unilateral announcement the USSR would cease deployment to the Mediterranean of some or all of the above. o Proposing mutual reductions in naval presence--measured in ship days--in areas such as the Mediterranean, Indian Ocean or South China Sea. Alternatively, he could unilaterally announce a Soviet cutback in out-of-area naval activity. o Suspending one or more major naval shipbuilding programs, such as the Tbilisi-class carrier or the Oscar-class cruise missile submarine. The Soviet carrier program is expensive. and an initiative in this area would put pressure on the United States to justify its unilateral superiority in large aircraft carriers. o Unilaterally give up, or offer to trade, Soviet naval bases and basing rights in the Third World, including: Soviet naval units at the resupply and repair facility at Tartus, Syria; naval reconnaissance missions occasionally flown out of Libya; the major Soviet air and naval base at Cam Ranh in Vietnam; and the Soviet repair facility on Dahlak Island off Ethiopia. Re CFE. Gorbachev could make proposals on CFE to promote his plans for a European summit in the latter half of 1990, set the stage for follow-on talks, or take advantage of the meeting's European setting to make regional proposals. He might point to the ongoing instability in East Germany as underlining the need for urgency and compromise to quickly reduce tensions in the region. Some possible candidates: 10 s (oy)

o An offer to withdraw more Soviet forces from Hungary, particularly aircraft, in return for abandonment of NATO plans to rebase F-16s from Spain to Italy. o A joint compromise on two outstanding problems, aircraft and personnel, whereby the West would agree to accept Pact proposals on personnel reductions in return for Soviet acceptance of NATO's position on non-exclusion of combat/combat-capable aircraft. 9 Alternatively, Gorbachev could offer an aircraft deal in which the Soviets accept inclusion of air defense interceptors and combat-capable trainers in return for Western agreement to exclude intercontinental bombers, land-based naval air, and unarmed trainers. [° On SNF, Gorbachev could weil reiterate Soviet appeals for immediate talks on a "mandate" for negotiations on SNF reductions. He could buttress this appeal with one or more of the following: o Unilateral reductions in Soviet/Pact SRBMs; o A proposal for deep reductions in Soviet/Pact SRBMs in return for much smaller US/NATO cuts: o Removal of all Soviet SRBMs from Eastern Europe and/or the European USSR in return for US removal of Lance and abandonment of follow-on to Lance. o As a grandstand move, propose a nuclear-free zone from the Atlantic to the Urals. He would assume this would be rejected out of hand by the United States as well as France. | Gorbachev could wrap some or all of his proposals around a suggestion to create a Soviet-American task force to settle CFE issues bilaterally. Moscow believes US-Soviet 11

pate discussions are the quickest way to achieve a CFE agreement and may think the United States is willing to go around its allies to get a quick accord. Ge Nuclear Testing. The nuclear testing treaties are all but finished, and both sides have said they would be ready to sign them at the spring Washington summit. Gorbachev is eager to move on to discussions of a comprehensive test ban and could --S__--r--rpush to sign the treaties now, hoping to ensure a visible success in December and use the occasion to urge further talks on a comprehensive test ban. He might bolster this by announcing another halt to Soviet testing, especially in light of growing public protest in the USSR over the environmental impact of underground tests. Chemical Weapons. Gorbachev might announce the USSR was proceeding with immediate unilateral destruction of its chemical weapons. In light of the recent closing of the Soviet chemical destruction facility at Chapayevsk he could ask for US technical assistance in destroying Soviet chemical stocks. Gorhachev will express disappointment over US proposals made at the UN in September, especially the-decision ta continue binary production, calling this incompatible with a global ban. He will urge that the two Se sides accelerate their discussions for deep bilateral reductions in stockpiles even before a multilateral treaty is signed. [°° Missile Proliferation. Missile proliferation in ine Middle East is of particular concern to the Soviet Union given the area's instability. the proliferation of chemical and nuclear warheads, and the proximity of many Middle Easrern countries to the USSR. Gorbachev could propose mutual constraints on supplying missiles and missile technology. He will be especially concerned about US assistance to Israel and may raise the Israeli Arrow ATBM program that has been partially funded by SDIO. He might propose a new multilateral grouping to supercede the Missile Technology Control Regime, which the Soviets see as Western-dominated. [°) 12

Eastern Europe and the German Question Gorbachev may use the December meeting to publicize this approach and to press both publicly and privately for the US position on the German question. Gorbachev may try to finesse the tricky question of the Soviet position on German reunification by subsuming it in a discussion of the long-term future for Europe. For instance, he might argue that resolving the issue now is premature, and suggest that it be postponed until the basic framework for a new European security order is in place. The surprise could be that Gorbachev might spring a vision of such an order including the pullout of all foreign troops--a goal frequently set for the year 2000--and the conclusion of follow-on talks to CFE. He might say that the Warsaw Pact is prepared to dissolve by a certain date, and challenge NATO to step up to this timetable. German reunification could be put on the table as a natura! byproduct of such a solution to East-West tensions. He might suggest that one step on the way to realizing this package would be removal of the Berlin Wall and talks to end the partition of Berlin, and hint ihac this could occur sooner rather than later, Given the present uncertainty and disorder affecting the East German government. Gorbachev almost certainly would not make 4 major announcement to the President regarding Germany without first consulting the East Germans. It is remotely possible, however, that Gorbachev could work out a program with the East German leadership prior to his meeting with the President. Such a program would be intended primarily to boister the legitimacy of the East German regime, and might include a decision to demolish the Berlin Wall in the near term. Although Gorbachev would almost certainly not take the lead in publicly announcing such a move, he mignt preview it privately with the President as a way of reinforcing the Soviet commitment to liberalization in Eastern Europe. ee Brezhnev Doctrine. Gorbachev could chose the Mediterranean meeting as a venue 13 Secreria

toemphasize the non-existence of the Brezhnev doctrine by renouncing the 1968 invasion of Czechoslovakia. This would go far to reassure both Eastern and Western audiences that the Brezhnev Doctrine was finally dead. Soviet assertions that intervention has no role in politics continue to be viewed suspiciously as long as the 1968 invasion gets official Soviet support, and there have already been reports that a renunciation was in the offing. This would be a body biow to the current Czech regime's "go-siow" approach, something Moscow might prefer given its positive attitude towards East European reforms. Warsaw Pact. Gorbachev could announce that Hungary was free to leave the Warsaw Pact after a CFE treaty. He might couple this with an invitation for some of NATO's smaller members to do likewise. This move could capitalize on what may be in the cards anyway, and could be used to put pressure on NATO to speed up CFE or its follow-on. (2% eo Regional Issues Gorbachev could propose ambitious 'jS-Soviet political cooperation to solve Third World problems. going beyond the resolution of remaining confrontations. He might justify continuing Soviet support for Nicarayua and Afghanistan as unavoidable obligations incurred as a result of "Brezhnev-era" policies, but stress that they will not be repeated and should not stand in the way of expanded cooperation to reduce instability and prevent the proliferation of nuclear and chemical weapons in the Third World. Be Central America/Cuba. In an effort to remove Nicaragua as an irritant to US-Soviet bilateral relations, Gorbachev may announce that the USSR will make its "temporary moratorium" on arms deliveries to Managua permanent, dropping the Soviet insistence that this could only occur if Washington discontinued military aid to its Central American 14 Secret ES me

weerer! allies. Such an offer could be linked to a guarantee from the US that it drop economic sanctions against Managua and agree to full normalization of relations. The Soviets probably also would hope that this would strengthen their efforts to improve relations with Nicaragua's neighbors and would increase regional discontent with US military aid to Central America. |" Gorbachev could also announce that the USSR will withdraw its brigade from Cuba in the hope that this would reduce Cuba as a contentious issue in US-Soviet relations and remove an obstacle to improved US-Cuban relations. Such a move would be in keeping with Soviet efforts to reduce its military presence outside of the USSR for economic reasons, and Gorbachev may also hope that it will send a message to Fidel Castro that the USSR no longer considers the US to be a military threat to Cuba. A more remote possibility would be an announcement that the USSR will eliminate or drastically reduce future military aid to Cuba. Again. the Soviets would have very good economic reasons for doing this--Soviet military aid to Cuba totaled over $1.6 billion in 1988--out they would not want to jeopardize their important intelligence gathering operations, port, and landing rights in Cuba. ES On the other hand, Gorbachev may come into his meeting with President Bush frustrated that the US has not responced more positively to previous Soviet moves in the region, such as the "temporary Moratorium" on arms shipments and Shevardnadze's visit to Managua and Havana in which he plaved up Soviet support for a regional security agreement and free and fair elections in Nicaragua. He may dlame Washington for renewed fighting in Nicaragua and argue that Moscow cannot continue its arms moratorium while US-backed Contras threaten the Sandinistas. The Soviets may believe that since Washington continues to blame Moscow for shipments of arms to Nicaragua that arrive from Cuba they have little to lose by renewing their own direct shipments. xox) 15 5 Pe

S (Px9) Afghanistan. This is one issue where, given the unexpectedly strong showing of the Najubullah regime over the past year, Gorbachev is more likely to take a tough than a conciliatory approach. If he chooses to go beyond tough talk, Gorbachev may notify the President that Moscow is "raising the ante" on aid to Kabul, to include provision of MiG-29 aircraft, T-72 tanks or SS-21 missiles. The Soviet leader would want to emphasize that the USSR will not back away from commitments to continue its ally's hold on power, particularly in a country literally in its backyard. By raising the possibility of an arms race in the area, Moscow would hope to bring home to the US and Pakistan that the conflict is unwinnable and too costly over the long run. Gorbachev couid raise the possibility of expanded aid to Kabul to reinforce a proposal to link Afghanistan and Central America: continuation of the claimed cutoff of arms deliveries to Nicaragua in return for US agreement to stop supplying arms to the Afghan resistance. Re Alternatively, Gorbachev could take steps to galvanize a peace process in Afghanistan by announcing Moscow s willingness to conduct talks, without any preconditions, with the resistance. Although this would be a significant departure from the Soviets' demand that Kabul participate in negotiations for a follow-on government, Gorbachev might find the prospect "f a long-term, resource-draining stalemate in Afghanistan too costly for domestic reasons. He might calculate, too, that some PDPA members are likely to be chosen as good Muslims" to participate in a government, thus providing Moscow with an ongoing channel in Kabul for its views. To underline Soviet good faith, Gorbachev could announce he is withdrawing Scud missiles from Afghanistan or pledge a moratorium on supplying additional missiles. Though less likely, he might informally confirm comments made occasionally by lower-level Soviet officials to the effect that Najibullah might be removed as a token of Soviet sincerity in seeking a political solution. Arab-Israeli Peace Process. Given the tentative progress in establishing a dialogue 16 Seeret}™

between Israel and the Palestinians, Gorbachev might state his intention to issue a formal invitation for Israeli Labor Party feader and Finance Minister Peres--or even other Israeli leaders--to visit Moscow. As the concept for Israeli-Palestinian talks has been brokered primarily by Egypt and the United States, the Soviet goal for this move would be to reinsert themselves as a major player in the peace process and prove faithful to previous statements that Moscow is seeking a "balance of interests" in the region. Gorbachev may also provide more explicit terms under which the USSR would reestablish relations with israel, perhaps linking the diplomatic opening to the beginning of an Israeli-Palestinian--rather than an Israeli-PLO--dialogue. Economic Issues Putting economic and trade matters at the center of summit discussions would itself be a major surprise. Gorbachey mav judge the time is right. however, for a push in an area that has received little top-ievel attention and where relatively small moves could have large symbolic importance. (2 Moscow's top priorities would be to entice the United States to expand its purchases of Soviet products and engage the United States in making the Soviet economic system more compatible with participation in the world economy. Although the Soviets have little new to offer unilaterally, there are a few tantalizing concessions they might hold out, such as: 3.3(0)() (eR eR: see ee ee ee 17 Secret SS

that have been a sticking point regarding Soviet entry into Western organizations such as GATT. The release of this data would be part of a campaign to facilitate Soviet economic integration with the West. ("| Especially surprising would be a request for US government assistance--disguised as special loan credits, trade subsidies or barter arrangments--to help the USSR's deteriorating economy and ease worsening shortages of consumer goods. Most extreme would be a plea for a Marshall Plan type of emergency aid package.( Other A number of plausible proposals are grouped here that did not fit neatly into any other category. More Defense Data. Gorbachev could present some dramatic package of Soviet military and economic data to the West in order to influence Western decisions and shane the image of the USSR. For instance, in the aftermath of the Supreme Soviet's budget deliberations, Gorbachev couid present details of defense spending and procurement, perhaps for the next five years. The intent would be convince Western audiences of the Soviet willingness to mouerate the military threat that they pose, and their desire to inject predictability into 'ne East-West arms relationship. The Soviets might give early indications about their release of military spending data in order to heighten Western expectations. BOE Closer Cooperation. Two other proposals would push US-Soviet cooperation into new areas: o Gorbachev could propose joint intelligence-counterintelligence cooperation in areas such as terrorism, drug trafficking, missile technology proliferation, or nuclear proliferation. 19 s pe:

o The Soviets may propose a major joint manned space mission, involving the US and Soviet space shuttles or the Mir space station. Gorbachev could appeal to the enormous cost of space exploration when borne by one country and stress the symbolic benefits of such a high-visibility project. Bye Blank Spots. In line with Shevardnadze's recent admissions on the Krasnoyarsk radar and Afghanistan, Gorbachev might offer explanations or apologies for past Soviet actions. He could bring up the KAL 007 shoatdown as an example of how past mistrust and unnecessary military tensions can lead to disaster. Although it would be directed more at Eastern Europe than the United States, he could formally acknowledge a Soviet role in the Katyn Forest massacre of Polish officers and issue guarantees that Soviet politics will never allow a repetition. ee Implications for the United States Though it is too soon to characterize Gorbachev as desperate---and one of his major goals during his Oecember meeting is likely to be to convey a sense that he remains in command--the tremendous pressure of events at home and in Eastern Europe is reinforcing his natural penchant for the dramatic. To the extent that Gorbachev sees his position threatened by Western unwillingness to meet him halfway in arms control negotiations or otherwise support perestroyka and East European reforms, he could be tempted to swing either towards threats ('Apres moi...) or pleas for help. His underlying goal could be to make Western governments look increasingly churlish in the eyes of Public opinion for refusing to cooperate, especially if Gorbachev is ousted or is compelled himself to backtrack on reforms. It is this strategy of entangling the United States in the fate of Gorbachev and perestroyka that poses the main challenge to US interests. ES In addition, initiatives on arms control could pose some specific problems for US 20 Seerer

, policy: o Dramatic proposals for deep reductions in strategic forces are designed to back the United States into a corner on SDI, cruise missiles, and other strategic modernization programs. © Proposals on naval arms control could appeal so strongly to US European allies that they cause friction in the alliance and lead to calls for US participation. CON BENTH AL "CONFEDENTTAB 8710 NATIONAL SECURITY COUNCIL WASHINGTON, D.C. 20506 wena ya cupe™ uy Tee Reo November 8, 1989 INFORMATION e MEMORANDUM FOR BRENT SCOWCROFT vy) ey ae ate Atsor FROM: WILLIAM L. STEARMAN \{O- seen SUBJECT: Soviet Summit Objectives Gorbachev most likely will play his December 2-3 meeting with the President as a full-fledged summit with primary emphasis on START with a possible surprise offer and with some linkage to U.S. naval reductions. He probably will not want to dwell much on Eastern Europe or on regional problems. On October 23, Shevardnadze told the Supreme Soviet: "Ahead lies a new Soviet-American summit meeting" at which "an agreement on strategic offensive arms" would, in effect, be "first and foremost" on the agenda. He was clearly referring to the December 2-3 meeting, of which he was already privy. Later in a TASS interview, Shevardnadze stated: "I am confident that despite its unofficial character, the Mediterranean meeting will be of milestone importance. It will largely determine the importance and substance of the official summit talks to be held late in spring-early in summer next year." (Pravda, November 6, 1989). On November 2, TANJUG quoted from an (Italian) ADNCRONOS-NEWS interview with Shevardnadze in which he reportedly said that the upcoming Mediterranean summit could "serve to lay the foundations for an agreement on a 50 percent reduction of strategic arsenals of the two big powers." In raising START, Gorbachev obviously will further insist that Soviet adherence to START reductions will depend on strict U.S. compliance with the ABM Treaty. (Continued Soviet concern about SDI is well reflected in Lt. General V. Starodubov's article in Krasnaya Zvezda of October 24, 1989). In addition, Gorbachev may well come up with a dramatic (at least in a PR sense) proposal on START. (I trust our arms control people are conjuring up all possible scenarios for such a Soviet surprise.) It also seems likely that Gorbachev will exploit the naval setting of the summit to press again for naval reductions and attendant confidence building measures. A substantial reduction in the U.S. Navy is a principal Soviet arms reduction goal. This promotes the broad Soviet objectives of decoupling the U.S. from Europe, of reducing the U.S. SLBM and SLCM threat to the USSR and ~CONFIDENTTAE- Declassify on: OADR -PANABAY HAL PER E.O. 13526 Leto~ 280b-wR Rirfrole WN

-CONPIDENPIAR 2 of weakening U.S. force projection potential. In his October 26 Helsinki speech, Gorbachev noted START progress, but warned: ",.. the more progress made on this path, the more pressing the need to resolve questions concerning naval forces, including confidence-building measures and corresponding verification [kontrol]. In the conditions of reducing other types of armaments, the destabilizing role of these forces will be increasing... the process of strengthening trust, reducing and then subsequently completely eliminating military confrontation, and making the transition to nonoffensive defense will simply not take place unless this problem is resolved." In the October 30 Pravda, Marshal Akhromeyev, elaborating on Gorbachev's Helsinki remarks, stated: "... naval forces constitute one of the most important problems and today even the most important problem, of the Soviet Union's security." He then goes on to say that with accords reached on 50 percent START reductions and on a radical force reduction in Europe "and if we actually reduce these forces on a reciprocal basis, while U.S. naval forces and U.S. naval bases surrounding the USSR remain intact, the military threat for the USSR will increase and the position of the Soviet Union in the world will deteriorate. How could we agree to this?" Two days later, a Radio Moscow commentator lamented that "the Western press is unjustifiably ignoring the Soviet leader's remarks in Helsinki" and that "the problem of naval forces is being passed over in silence by Western politicians as well." Gorbachev at the summit can certainly counter this indifference. Gorbachev may also raise other aspects of conventional arms reductions as well as an agreement on chemical weapons and a ban on nuclear weapons tests. In discussing internal developments in the USSR and Eastern Europe, Gorbachev will resist any attempt by the President to establish any kind of political droit de regard in Eastern Europe, but will welcome U.S. and other external economic assistance'to Eastern Europe, specifically to Poland and Hungary. He will most likely push for lowering barriers to Western technology transfer to the USSR and for improved trade ties on more favorable (to the USSR) terms. The general line Gorbachev will take on developments in the USSR and Eastern Europe will probably resemble the following remarks by commentator Stanislav Kondrashov in the November 6 Izvestiya: "If the progress of perestroyka has determined favorable changes in the international arena, its failure is quite possibly fraught with the return and -CONFTPENTEAD

-CONFIDENFIAL 3 intensification of the state of military confrontation, with the concomitant danger of the renewal of a ruinous arms race of increased savagery. As far as the East European countries are concerned, particularly Poland and Hungary, which are now being paid such close, interested attention by those across the ocean and by Western capitals in general, their development along the path of democratic pluralism is also linked to the development of democratic processes in the Soviet Union and the evolution of the Warsaw Pact from a military-political grouping into a politicalmilitary alliance, in which the free voice of every participant will grow." Gorbachev will have little interest in discussing regional affairs save for a possible pitch for regional zones of reduced armaments (or nuclear-free). On Nicaragua, he will probably again call for joint U.S.-Soviet oversight of external military involvement in the area. At the risk of stating the obvious, I would note for the record that summitry for the Soviets remains, first and foremost, an exercise in public relations to generate pressure on the U.S. to accept Soviet negotiating positions and to promote a spirit of detente aimed at enticing the West to lower its guard. Soviet media commentators portray the President as being under considerable pressure from the Democrats and our allies to come to terms with the Soviets and indicate that is why he has now agreed to a summit with Gorbachev -- especially since the latter has already met with Thatcher, Kohl and Mitterrand. Gorbachev no doubt calculates he can exploit this pressure at the summit. The first postwar summit, in 1955, produced the "Spirit of Geneva." The Soviets, including Shevardnadze, are now touting the "Spirit of Wyoming." The detente spirit now being promoted was well characterized by Shevardnadze (in the above quoted TASS interview) when he cited the November 3 U.S.-Soviet joint UN resolution and the upcoming summit as showing "again the possibility and feasibility of cooperation of the USSR and the US in international affairs during the development of constructive parallelism of Soviet-American and multilateral interaction..."
