BLACKWILL (b SUBJECT: Possible U.S. Initiatives at the Malta Meeting I want to be sure that you know that I am now doing no interagency work to prepare possible U.S. initiatives to be used by the President at Malta. Here is, nevertheless, my short list on the subject. 1. Economic -- Announce that the two sides will immediately begin negotiations on a U.S.-Soviet trade agreement after which the President will seek a waiver of Jackson-Vanik. -- Announce details of further U.S. Technical "Assistance" to the Soviet Union (or "Technical Cooperation" as the Soviets would prefer to call it.) -- Agree to support Soviet observer status at the GATT. 2. Arms Control -- Present a deMIRVing initiative. -- Seek Soviet agreement to the President's UN CW initiative in return for the U.S. foregoing CW modernization. -- Propose further U.S./Soviet reductions in CFE in the context of the changed situation in Europe and u.S. defense budget decisions. -- Agree that the two leaders will attend a CFE Summit to sign an agreement. -SECREP/ SENSITIVE Declassify on: OADR PER E.O. 13526 2.0 10- 1LB0(6-MR wfr2!roie MX

SEEREP 2 3. Procedural -- Set dates for the 1990 Summit. -- Elaborate an intense Ministerial work program in the next months to prepare for the Summit. 4. Human Rights -- Propose that the Soviet Union open its borders. 5. Regional Issues 6. The Environment 7. Space Please let me know if your wish me to do anything to push any of these possible initiatives along. SEGREP/SENSITIVE

SECREP- RECORD ID: 8910474 NSC/S PROFILE RECEIVED: 29 DEC 89 20 TO: SCOWCROFT FROM: KANTER DOC DATE: 24 NOV 89 BLACKWILL SOURCE REF: KEYWORDS: MALI SUMMIT ARMS CONTROL CFE USSR PERSONS: GORBACHEV, MIKHAIL S SUBJECT: POSSIBLE INITIATIVES IN THE CONTEXT OF MALTA ACTION: ORIGINALS RETURNED TO NSC/S DUE DATE: 02 JAN 90 STATUS: C STAFF OFFICER: KANTER LOGREF: FILES: PA NSCP: CODES: DOCUMENT DISTRIBUTION FOR ACTION FOR _CONCURRENCE FOR INFO White House Guidelines E.O, 12958, SEC 3.4 (B) September 11, 2006 By Gp__ NARA, Date Shirley COMMENTS: DISPATCHED BY DATE BY HAND W/ATTCH OPENED BY: NSRLG CLOSED BY: NSRLG poc 10F 1

iy ae 10474 sHoES & mont pentose NATIONAL SECURITY COUNCIL WASHINGTON, D.C. 20506 November 24, 1989 Hen INFORMATION MEMORANDUM FOR BRENT SCOWCROFT Re FROM: ARNOLD coal tay Roper? D. BLACKWILL SUBJECT: Possible Initiatives in the Context of Malta Following on your Wednesday breakfast discussion with Secretaries Baker and Cheney, we met this morning with Reg Bartholomew, Paul Wolfowitz, and Howard Graves in a principals-only "little five" meeting. We described our task as identifying and assessing various ways to get something from the Soviets for the defense budget cuts we probably will be making in any event. We stressed the obvious sensitivity of the enterprise, that there would be zero tolerance for leaks, and that the information had to be strickly limited to the people in room and their bosses. In that spirit, no papers were circulated in advance or carried away from the meeting. The session was confined to talking through various possibilities, with the two of us undertaking to write an issues paper based on the discussion. That paper is attached. It has not been shared with anyone. We plan to reconvene the group tomorrow (Saturday) afternoon to go over it--after Wolfowitz and Graves return from Camp David--making whatever changes are necessary or desirable. After that, we will give you the revised version to work with your counterparts and the President in the run up to Malta. One complication is that Cheney and Powell are scheduled to leave for Europe Saturday evening, returning next Thursday. Wolfowitz and Graves may be designated as their stand-ins, but you should confirm this on the margins of your Camp David meeting tomorrow morning. Reading between the lines in today's meeting, it appears as though Cheney and Powell may resist all efforts to advance any concrete ideas or initiatives at Malta. They are inately suspicious of any such approach, worry about the appearance or reality of "Reykjavik II," and argue that we are running serious risks of allowing Malta to force decisions about the defense program and budget that have yet to be made. Based on a report to him about out meeting this morning, Cheney is said to believe that our effort to assess possible initiatives is a "bad idea." oRBe- DECLASSIFIED Declassify on: OADR ---- AS AMENDED: SEGRET AS AMEND @y Fliz\o4

SReRET "2: Their preferred approach is suggested by the Wolfowitz memo distributed at the Wednesday breakfast: take a broad, philosophical approach that tells the Soviets what we expect of them, and drops hints about what we would be prepared to do if and as they actually meet our expectations. The problems with this approach are that the Soviets might well not understand what we are talking about, and might well not take us seriously if they did. Given our defense budget realities, however, we will have to start reshaping our forces immediately, whether or not the Soviets live up to the standards we set for them. Reg seems much more willing to contemplate various ways in which to accelerate a CFE agreement in order to get some benefit from our looming defense cuts. He is very concerned (and we think appropriately) about the impact on our Allies of anything that smacks of a superpower deal on CFE. None of the agency principals appeared to have considered possible initiatives other than those related to CFE. We briefly discussed ideas related to START, CW, defense spending, naval arms control, and ASAT, and these are included in the paper. We would appreciate getting any reactions to the paper or other guidance you may have before you leave for Camp David Saturday morning. As noted above, we are scheduled to meet with the three others at 2:30 on Saturday to go over the paper again. Attachment Issues Paper

SECREE CHANGES IN THE DEFENSE PROGRAM AND POSSIBLE INITIATIVES IN THE CONTEXT OF MALTA The U.S. defense program probably will undergo a significant restructuring and substantial reductions over the next few years. This paper assesses alternative ways in which to portray certain of these prospective defense program changes in the context of the President's meeting with Gorbachev at Malta. Whatever we do in this regard will bear directly on Allied interests and their concerns that Malta might be a "Reykjavik II." We face three broad choices about how to accommodate these allied sensitivities. (a) Consult with the Allies prior to Malta and shape an approach to Gorbachev in light of those consultations. (b) Sound out Gorbachev at Malta about various initiatives and report to Allies on results at the December 4 NAC in Brussels. (c) Take a broad, philosophical approach at Malta that does not result in any specific commitments or undertakings. Each of these alternatives has significant disadvantages: (a) Consulting with the Allies prior to Malta would risk leaks, constrain the President's flexibility, and aggravate fears that the meeting had become recast into a Summit-like negotiation. (b) Trying out ideas on Gorbachev without prior Allied consultations could seem to the Allies as though they had been misled about the purpose of Malta, that the superpowers had cut deals over their heads, and that they were being informed of a fait accompli. (c) Taking a broad, philosophical approach could leave Gorbachev uncertain about what we expected of him and what we would be prepared to do in return. It could seem as though we were setting high standards for Soviet conduct, but subsequently be seen to take actions unilaterally (because of budget pressures) before those standards had been met. secret AS AMENDED. -- wy 19°R\-O1 A pre oe: BU siiog

SECREP 2 CFE Overview The purpose here is to examine whether we can use CFE to try to get something from the Soviets for cuts in U.S. force structure that we will be forced to make anyway because of the budget situation, and at the same time to accelerate the conclusion of a CFE agreement. (DOD budget plans could require the withdrawal of 30,000 troops from Europe in FY 91 even if there is no CFE agreement, and a cut of U.S. ground and air forces in Europe by perhaps 150,000 by FY 94.) The options examined below would: (1) drop aircraft and helicopters from CFE I and seek to persuade Moscow to agree quickly, perhaps by April 1990, to a Treaty that covers only tanks, armored troop carriers and artillery. This would allow us to make major savings in the period ahead by both rapidly implementing the 30,000 man European cut, and more importantly, by reducing CONUS forces in anticipation of the very large Soviet equipment reductions embodied in such a CFE I Treaty. We would assure the Soviets that we would begin CFE II expeditiously in order to respond to their expressed concerns about Western aircraft and helicopters; and/or (2) offer to lower the ceiling on U.S. and Soviet troops from 275,000 to 200,000 in CFE I, or alternatively and more to Moscow's liking, promise now to do so as a subceiling in CFE II, while at the same time meeting Soviet concerns by NATO agreeing now to broaden the manpower ceiling in CFE II to cover all indigenous and stationed troops in the Central Region. The Soviets will find it hard to drop aircraft and helicopters from CFE I without any concessions from our side, although their budget problems might induce a change in their position. Winning Allied support for any new CFE initiative would require extraordinary efforts at the political level to duplicate the exceptional consensus achieved at the NATO Summit in May. Finally, getting a CFE agreement signed quickly will trigger soon thereafter the beginning of SNF negotiations well before the West German elections at the end of 1990, a fact that at a minimum will deeply concern Mrs. Thatcher. (Imagine Helmut Kohl's reaction if Gorbachev proposed a Third SNF Zero at the height of the West German election campaign.) Current Situation in Vienna Although East and West are still struggling over counting rules for tanks and armored troop carriers, there are no fundamental reasons why these categories cannot soon join artillery in the list of agreed definitions. Aircraft and helicopters present a

SECREP 3 different picture. Unless the East bends soon to the Western position or the West makes concessions, the two sides must defer these categories beyond CFE I if they are to gain quick agreement. Defer the Aircraft Category Until CFE IT Paradoxically, both the U.S. and the Soviet Union expect to benefit militarily from inclusion of combat aircraft in CFE reductions. From the U.S. perspective, two factors are paramount. -- First, the Warsaw Pact has many more combat aircraft that does NATO (over 14,000 to about 6700) and stands to take much greater cuts under the current Western proposal (common ceiling of 5,700). These cuts would tip the air balance decidedly toward the West, given our qualitative edge. = Second, NATO air forces are planning to reduce their aircraft inventories by about 15% unilaterally in any event, for budgetary reasons. So if we drop aircraft out of our CFE I proposal, the West would make these cuts without necessarily getting anything from the East in exchange. Nevertheless, the Soviet attitude toward deferring this category could well be negative, though perhaps somewhat ambivalent. Like us, they would favor moves that would simplify the CFE I negotiation and improve chances for an early agreement, not least because an SNF negotiation would follow close behind. But inclusion of aircraft was an Eastern proposal that the West had long opposed, until we changed our position in May. To some extent, the Soviets are now the prisoners of their own rhetoric. For more than three years, in a determined effort to portray the conventional military situation in Europe as balanced, they have argued that Western advantages in aircraft and helicopters offset admitted Eastern superiority in ground combat items like tanks. This argument was based, in part, ona genuine Soviet concern about the capacity of superior Western aircraft to influence results on the battlefield. But the Eastern fear never had a firm basis in actual aircraft numbers. Moscow this spring distorted counting rules in tabling their proposal in Vienna on aircraft so that the West would appear to have more of the "tactical strike" aircraft that the East wanted to cut. Soviet CFE representations are now putting forward compromise ideas that would entail much larger Pact aircraft cuts. Nevertheless, for both military and public diplomacy reasons, the Soviets -- even at the top levels -- could find it difficult to accept a CFE I agreement that left Western air capabilities wholly unconstrained. With our continuing rejection

SBERET 4 of naval arms control, making NATO air forces immune from immediate cuts as well could seem, from the Soviet perspective, to mean that conventional arms control only emasculated Soviet strength while ignoring Western advantages. Our allies' views would be more mixed. The British and the French never liked the idea of including aircraft in CFE I, at least in part because of their dual use of conventional combat aircraft as nuclear delivery systems. The Germans, on the other hand, will accept any idea, whether to include aircraft or exclude them, that will accelerate conclusion of a CFE I agreement and the beginning of SNF negotiations. Since the NATO Summit, however, all of our allies have become more aware, and more interested, in the apparent prospect of asymmetrical Eastern reductions in combat aircraft. Defer the Helicopter Cagetory Until CFE IT The best, and fastest, way to overcome current disagreements on helicopters would be to drop the category from CFE I. Both sides now agree, in principle, on an overall ceiling of 1900 combat helicopters. They disagree on how to count combat helicopters. The problem here is most difficult for NATO because it now seems that sticking to the 1900 figure could force NATO to accept deep (30% or more) cuts, causing bitter intra-alliance disputes over the allocation of these reductions. Few in NATO would object to dropping this category. The most important military disadvantage is that it would leave an important Warsaw Pact capability unconstrained in CFE I, with the possibility, in the absence of a CFE II treaty, of competition in helicopters supplanting competition in ground attack fixed-wing aircraft. On the other hand, the Warsaw Pact would not start such a competition from a position of great advantage. There are about 1,200 Pact attack helicopters and about 900+ on the NATO side, and a number of NATO countries have active procurement programs underway to modernize and expand their helicopter strength. The Soviets can be expected to object to dropping helicopters out of CFE I. As in the case of aircraft, Moscow originally proposed inclusion of this category, arguing that it was an area of NATO strength which counterbalanced Pact ground force advantages. Asking the Soviets to drop both aircraft and helicopters from CFE I_with nothing in return from the West would be a very big pill for them to swallow, undoing what they thought they had gained from the President's May initiative. Propose Deeper U.S.-Soviet Manpower Cuts SeCRED

SECRET S We currently propose U.S.-Soviet reductions so that each country would have no more than 275,000 ground and air personnel stationed on foreign soil in Europe. This implies a reduction of 30,000 U.S. troops and about 320,000 Soviets. To move beyond these reductions to a U.S.-Soviet stationed ceiling of, say, 200,000 would mean that the U.S. would probably have to rethink its strategy for defending the inter-German frontier in two ways: (1) reconsider how many U.S. units need to be forward deployed in peacetime, given the declining threat of a short warning attack, and the effect of this change on our reinforcement requirements; and (2) redistribute the remaining U.S. forces in the FRG, perhaps radically thinning out the corps now deployed on the Bavarian-Czech border. Ceilings lower than 200,000 would force the U.S. to alter the fundamental nature of its commitment to NATO, and would almost certainly invalidate the strategy of forward defense as it is currently understood. Merely to have a trained U.S. infrastructure in place on the Continent for rapid reinforcement requires a substantial number of personnel. If these are maintained, ceilings on U.S. forces in Europe of 150,000 or 100,000 would leave less and less room for forward deployed active units. The problem we would have with Moscow on this score in CFE I might not only be with the effect of this 200,000 U.S.-Soviet ceiling on Soviet interests in Eastern and Central Europe. The ceiling would not include other stationed forces, or other indigenous forces. The Bundeswehr would therefore be unconstrained and well over 400,0000 in strength, a dark and forboding shadow from the Soviet Union's past. Other implications of a 200,000 U.S. ceiling in CFE I could be: -- The allies may worry that we are leaving Europe. Many of them, especially Mrs. Thatcher, believe our troops are there in current circumstances in large part to help manage the Germans. -- U.S. cuts could therefore seriously unsettle the center of Europe at a particularly delicate time. -- Larger U.S. and Soviet reductions in the near term could trigger unilateral cuts across NATO which, along with the increasingly difficulty of modernizing U.S. nuclear weapons on the Continent, could badly weaken the credibility of Flexible Response. = Such an initiative in CFE I could altogether ruin the President's May NATO Summit triumph, breaking apart the Allied

SEGREP 6 solidarity he cemented together. In order to make this idea more palatable to the Allies, and more interesting to the Soviets, one could propose now that these additional U.S. troop reductions be made in a rapidly negotiated CFE II as part of a broader manpower ceiling on all forces in Central Europe. Include all Manpower in the Central Region Another way to implement steeper U.S. troop cuts might be in connection with a general manpower ceiling in CFE II for the Central Region, including both indigenous and stationed forces. The ceiling could include a subceiling for a U.S. and Soviet manpower. This approach would resemble the old NATO position in MBFR. Grinevsky floated just such an idea with regard to CFE I in his recent meeting with Woolsey, suggesting a 275,000 U.S.- Soviet subceiling and a 570,000 total for the Central Region (which would include the FRG, the Benelux countries, and possibly Denmark on the Western side and the GDR, Poland, Czechoslovakia, and possibly Hungary in the East). We could structure a proposal in CFE II for a ceiling on U.S. and Soviet forces of 200,000, and a common manpower ceiling for each side in the Central Region -- including Denmark and Hungary. This would both allow our allies to participate in reductions during CFE II, and meet Soviet concerns about the size of the Bundeswehr. The Soviets would consider inclusion of the Bundeswehr in NATO's troop ceilings to be a major Western concession, and attach a particular premium to this move given recent events in the GDR. It is unclear whether this would be enough to convince Moscow to go ahead with CFE I without aircraft and helicopters and to accept a U.S.-Soviet manpower ceiling of 275,000. Since the main impact of the additional ceiling would be the inclusion of the Bundeswehr, it is noteworthy that the FRG negotiators in Vienna praised Grinevsky's idea for an overall Central European manpower ceiling last week in a private meeting with Woolsey, saying this option would be best from the standpoint of German internal politics and in light of planned unilateral cuts in the Bundeswehr. The West could explain that it had singled out the Central Region for a manpower ceiling in CFE II in order to find a solution to the disagreement with the East on how to limit Soviet manpower in Eastern Europe, stressing the fact that the Soviet ceiling would be even lower under this scheme. There are also the usual arguments about the special salience of the Central Region in the SBCRET

SBERET 7 East-West military balance. The West could also assert, with some accuracy, that while troop levels are inherently difficult to verify, bounding the problem in this way and in this region offered the best prospects for effective verification of a manpower ceiling. Among the disadvantages of this option are that, because of the inclusion of French troops and because of the resemblance to MBFR, France might veto. (The FRG, however, has noted that the French may have already anticipated a move to broaden the manpower ceiling in deciding not to deploy their portion of the joint Franco-German brigade in Germany.) Flank states could question the emphasis in CFE II on the center, although Turkey, for one, would not be anxious to be included in manpower ceilings. Other Impediments to a CFE I Agreement If we were able to resolve East-West differences on aircraft, helicopters, and manpower in CFE I, the most difficult obstacles blocking an agreement would have been overcome. The related issues of stored equipment and delineation of the subzones are already moving closer to resolution. There would, however, be an inevitable process of nailing down an agreed treaty text, agreeing on lists of covered systems, drawing up needed annexes, and working out an agreed regime for verification and stabilizing measures (the two sides are close here in principle but the details are numerous and complex). The toughest challenges would probably be within the Alliance. On verification, for example, we may find it easier to agree on a regime for production monitoring with the Soviets than with Italy. Even if there is broad agreement on substance, we have seen how, for the last two weeks, the Greeks have stood alone in blocking tabling of a Western treaty text for domestic political reasons. And in any event, a CFE I Treaty negotiated at this pace would inevitably contain much less stringent verification than has earlier been the case. . Chemical Weapons Background Approximately $800 million is included in the defense program between now and FY 1994 for binary modernization. The cost to the year 2000 is projected to be approximately $3 billion. The need to reduce defense spending could lead to cuts--perhaps substantial--in the binary modernization program. Separately, SEGRES

SSGRED 8 Congress could impose sharp spending limitations on binary CW, either in the context of overall cuts in the defense budget or due to opposition to the modernization program itself. In either event, the feasibility of modernizing the CW stockpile with binary agents, and especially the feasibility of deploying the Bigeye air-delivered munition, could be in doubt. The Soviets say they stopped producing CW in 1987, and challenged us to cease CW production immediately. They have not, however, responded to the President's proposal for bilaterial reductions down to 20 percent of the current U.S. stockpile, and have avoided engaging us in discussions about it. There is, of course, great uncertainty about the size of the current Soviet CW stockpile. Possible Initiative The U.S. could offer to end CW binary production before the modernizaton program is scheduled to be completed in exchange for Soviet endorsement of the President's CW initiative, viz., reductions to 2 percent of the current U.S. stockpile within eight years after the chemical weapons convention enters into force, preceded by bilateral reductions to 20 percent of current U.S. levels. The U.S. offer to cease CW production could be cast either in a multilateral or bilateral context: (a) multilateral: In exchange for Soviet agreement to the 20 percent/2 percent approach, agree to cease CW production as soon as the chemical weapons convention enters into force (an act that would require Soviet participation). (b) bilateral: In exchange for Soviet agreement to reduce to 20 percent of the U.S. stockpile, agree to cease CW production as soon as a Memorandum of Understanding is signed, preferably at the 1990 Summit. A variant of the bilateral alternative would be to combine this offer with a challenge to the Soviets to reduce our respective CW stockpiles down to 10 percent of current U.S. levels, i..e, well below the impact of the Congressionally-mandated unilateral reductions but within the range of a near-term binary production program. Such an initiative could take advantage of possible unilateral curtailment in our CW modernization program to help lock in Soviet agreement to the President's CW initiative. It also might help strengthen Congressional support for the program in the interim. On the other hand, it could sacrifice CW modernization that the budget and the Congress might otherwise allow. By

SBeRee 9 reversing course on CW modernization so soon after the President's UN speech, it also might be seen as vacillation or indecisiveness. The Chairman, JCS strongly opposes any CW initiative that would have the effect of limiting CW production. START and Strategic Force Modernization Background Although specific decisions have not been made about the post- START strategic force structure, several options are under consideration as part of the review of the defense program. These include downloading Minuteman III missiles to a 1-RV configuration and retiring Minuteman II. In combination with plans to redeploy Peacekeeper in the rail-garrison basing mode, this option would result in the phase out of silo-based MIRVed ICBMs. Congress made relatively minor reductions in the budget requests for rail-garrison Peacekeeper and SICBM in the FY 1990 budget that was just passed. Future Congressional support for both mobile ICBM programs remains uncertain. The defense program currently includes plans to build one Trident SSBN per year. Longer-term projections of this building rate would yield a fleet of about 30 Trident submarines by the year 2000 (well above any plausible START-constrained force structure). The long-lead items for the eighteenth Trident and the core for the nineteenth Trident are included in the justpassed FY 1990 defense budget. Budget pressures could lead to a reduced Trident building rate and/or a curtailed Trident fleet. Another consideration as the defense program is restructured is that START constraints will force tradeoffs between ICBM RVs and SLBM RVs. In the past, the Soviets have argued for the elimination of nuclear weapons. More recently, they have suggested that the two superpowers move to a "minimum deterrence" force, perhaps as an interim step to eliminating nuclear weapons entirely. Gorbachev could make a proposal along these lines at Malta. Possible Initiative The U.S. could propose to: (a) lower START limits from the current 6000 warheads by an SERED

SECRET 10 additional 20 percent to 4800 warheads, coupled with (b) a ban on all MIRVed ICBMs, both silo-based and mobile. In essence, we would offer to reduce START limits by trading our MIRVed Minuteman III and Peacekeeper missiles for the Soviet SS- 18 and SS-24 ICBMs. These MIRVed ICBMs could be phased out over the seven year timetable currently proposed for START reductions, or over a somewhat longer period. Under such a proposal, the U.S. ICBM force would consist of a combination of 800 SICBM and 1-RV Minuteman missiles, and 15 START-accountable Tridents (assuming that we continue to deploy 1100 START-accountable bombers and ALCMs). It would eliminate some of the most destabilizing systems in the nuclear arsenal, including the most threatening Soviet systems. It would also reduce our ability to cover the Soviet target base, and drive us in the direction of strategic nuclear systems that are relatively more expensive. SICBM would be critical to the continued viability of the ICBM leg of the Triad under this approach. It probably would be desirable to accelerate its IOC, but it is doubtful that the timetable could be appreciably shortened. If SICBM were not deployed for whatever reason, this proposal would be seen by many as tantamount to a U.S. decision to move to an "augmented dyad" for nuclear deterrence. Phase-out of Peacekeeper missiles might produce some near-term budget savings as a result of cancellation of the rail-garrison rebasing program, but would result in the elimination of our most modern and capable ICBM RVs. The implications of such a cancellation for continued Congressional support for SICBM are unclear. Under this approach, we would not fund any new starts for Trident submarines and probably would not spend some of the money in the FY 1990 and FY 1991 budgets for long-lead items. This proposal would have a much larger impact on the Soviets. They could reject it amid angry charges of American "grandstanding" at what was supposed to be an informal, no agenda meeting at Malta. They could well counter with proposals to ban SLCMs, or sharply limit or ban MIRVed SLBMs. Variants of the proposal could limit the MIRV ban either to all silo-based MIRVed missiles, or all mobile MIRVed missiles. A ban on mobile MIRVs would permit the U.S. to retain Peacekeeper missiles in silos (and allow the Soviet Union to retain its SS-18s and SS-24s). It would reduce the costs of strategic force modernization to the U.S., and provide a hedge against the uncertain prospects for SICBM. It would require us to explain

SECRET il how we reconcile our START emphasis on strategic stability with an approach that sanctioned vulnerable MIRVs and banned survivable MIRVs. It probably would be marginally more acceptable to the Soviets than would a total ban on MIRVed ICBMs. Assuming a force of 800 ICBM RVs, a ban on silo-based MIRVed ICBMs would permit the U.S. to proceed with rebasing Peacekeeper, and to deploy either 300 SICBMs or 300 1-RV Minuteman IIIs. The Soviets would have to eliminate all of their SS-18s and silobased SS-24s. It would not mitigate, and could aggravate, the verification and breakout risks posed by mobile MIRVs. The Soviets probably would find a ban on silo-based MIRVs more difficult to accept than a mobile MIRV ban unless they could be persuaded that the phase-out of their silo-based force was in their long term interest, or that U.S. concessions on other START issues, combined with a rapid conclusion of a START agreement, made it worthwhile. Naval Force Reductions and Naval Arms Control Background Although we have made clear that we categorically reject the idea, there is a widespread expectation that Gorbachev will propose naval arms control in some form or other at Malta. Budget pressures are likely to lead to some reductions in naval forces. Options under consideration include early retirement of two carriers, mothballing some or all of the battleships. Escorts would be reduced proportionally. Possible Initiative The U.S. would continue to reject all efforts to engage us in discussions or negotiations about naval arms control. We could respond to such proposals, however, by observing that: (a) U.S. requirements for naval forces depend upon threats to our interests around the world. (b) As those threats diminish, our requirements will be adjusted accordingly. (c) Reductions in Soviet military capabilities particularly those that are well-suited for offensive missions, are one of the things that would lead to lessened threats and lowered requirements for naval forces.

SBCRET 12 (d) Reductions in regional tensions and regional instabilities likewise would permit smaller U.S.naval forces. The Soviet Union could help us address such regional problems. Defense Spendin Background The U.S. defense budget has declined in real terms every year since FY 1985. Even deeper reductions are in prospect. There are some signs that trends in Soviet defense spending also may be turning down. Because of the vast disparities in the relative sizes of our two economies, a given level of Soviet defense spending consumes a much larger percentage of their GNP than an equivalent expenditure requires of ours. Possible Initiative The U.S. could challenge the Soviet Union to reduce our respective "defense burdens," measured as a percentage of GNP, by 15-20 percent. For the U.S., this would reduce the amount of the GNP devoted to defense by about 1 percent. For the Soviets, the reduction in GNP spent on defense probably would be in excess of 3 percent. ASAT The implications of defense budget cuts, if any, for our ASAT program are sufficiently uncertain that it does not seem worthwhile to raise the subject in any way at Malta. SEGRE?

Same 8333 NATIONAL SECURITY COUNCIL WASHINGTON, D.C. 20506 October 24, 1989 ACTION MEMORANDUM FOR BRENT SCOWCROFT THROUGH: ARNOLD KANTER AU 7 FROM: DON PILLING ( SUBJECT: Arms Control -- Savings and Costs In following up on the President's questions about when arms control will yield defense budget reductions, the memorandum at Tab I shows there is no "peace dividend" resulting from arms control. Indeed, verification costs could exceed what we hope to gain from all of our current arms control agenda. It concludes by showing just how dramatic a change is needed in policy and/or force structure to gain significant savings. RECOMMENDATION That you sign the memorandum to the President at Tab I. ; MITINT . > -CONEEPENETAEE 19253 CHRON FILE ~ THE WHITE HOUSE "2>, WASHINGTON oy December 28, 1989 "Oo INFORMATION Va Q> MEMORANDUM FOR THE PRESIDENT oy FROM: BRENT SCOWCROFT/ ~ ' * SUBJECT: Memorandum of Agreement for the INF Treaty On Thursday, December 21, 1989, the U.S. Representative to the Special Verification Commission (SVC) signed a Memorandum of Agreement (MOA) with his Soviet counterpart, which spells out detailed procedures for implementing certain provisions of the INF Treaty. The document is over 130 pages long, and covers the technical characteristics and methods of use of inspection equipment, logistical arrangements for inspections, and a host of other implementation questions. The length and level of detail indicate and the complexity of carrying out an arms control agreement, which has unprecedented on-site inspection provisions will thus be a case study for other arms control agreements we are negotiating. In fact, our proposal in START to negotiate and begin early implementation of certain verification measures is based on our INF experience. Although the MOA covers most of the outstanding implementation issues, there will be a number of additional details that will still have to be worked out. We have decided to go ahead and sign the MOA at this time because we want to codify those procedures that have been agreed upon, and which are being implemented on the ground. The remaining issues, and any new questions that arise, will be on the agenda for future SVC meetings as part of a process that will probably continue throughout the life of the Treaty. Senate approval of the MOA is not required, but as we agreed during the INF ratification proceedings, we will promptly brie: the Congress and send the text of the MOA, along with two Agre-: Statements relating to the portal monitoring provisions signec earlier this year. ce: Vice Preside: Paty ---- Chief of $t:: Same problem as Panama memo, thougl no one's sure if -CoNBipENY President saw this. CONEEDENTTAE 10253 NATIONAL SECURITY COUNCIL WASHINGTON, D.C. 20506, December 20, 1989 ACTION MEMORANDUM FOR BRENT SCOWCROFT THROUGH: ARNOLD KANTER MMO FROM: JUDYT renner tf SUBJECT: MOA for INF Treaty On Thursday, the U.S. Representative to the Special Verification Commission (SVC) will sign a Memorandum of Agreement (MOA) with his Soviet counterpart, which spells out detailed procedures for implementing certain provisions of the INF Treaty. The document is over 130 pages long, and covers the technical characteristics and methods of use of inspection equipment, logistical arrangements for inspections, and a host of other implementation questions. The length and level of detail indicate the complexity of carrying out an arms control agreement that has unprecedented on-site inspection provisions, and will thus be a case study for other arms control agreements we are negotiating. In fact, our proposal in START to negotiate and begin early implementation of certain verification measures is based on our INF experience. Although the MOA covers most of the outstanding implementation issues, there will be a number of additional details that will still have to be worked out. We have decided to go ahead and sign the MOA at this time because we want to codify those procedures that have been agreed upon, and which are currently being implemented on the ground. The remaining issues, and any new questions that arise, will be on the agenda for future SVC meetings as part of a process that will probably continue throughout the life of the Treaty. Senate approval of the MOA is not required, but as we agreed during the INF ratification proceedings, we will promptly brief the Congress and send the text of the MOA, along with two Agreed Statements relating to the portal monitoring provisions signed earlier this year. ACDA Congressional Affairs will make brief phone calls to the appropriate Committee chairmen and staffers to alert them to this. (The delegations in Geneva will handle the signing in a low-key manner, with a brief press statement.) 2 gain Soviet acknowledgment of our existing tights will place the resolution of the divergence between U.S. and Soviet views of the ABM Treaty on the U.S. Senate during the ratification of START. It is a strategy most likely to result in funding and legislative constraints on SDI, that could prevent or at least impede the accomplishment of the program's objectives. Moreover, it is a strategy which is least likely to afford the President a politically viable defense of his constitutional prerogative to interpret treaties. (p) Consequently, we should place the onus of "relinkage" where 'it belongs: on the Soviet side. We have begun to make this point in the recent instructions for Round XIII of the Defense and Space Talks, in which we state that SDI development and testing would not constitute a violation of the ABM Treaty. I am enclosing talking points for your consideration which make the larger point that compliance with the START Treaty will not be conditioned on the waiver or forfeit of U.S. rights under the ABM Treaty (TAB A). (3) In my view, there is at least a reasonable chance that the Soviet side will give us an explicit acknowledgement in the START endgame of our testing rights. Even if one were not confident of eventual Soviet acknowledgement, it would be damaging in the START ratification debate if the negotiating record were to show that, between the Wyoming Ministerial and the June Summit, we did not press our view of the right to conduct development and testing under the ABM Treaty. attachment a/s (| JL ec: Ambassador Burt t Ambassador Smith Seas ee ae

Moscow Ministerial Talking Points Defense and Space -- At the 1987 Washington Summit, President Reagan and General Secretary Gorbachev agreed to the phrase "research, development and testing as required which are permitted by the ABM Treaty as signed in 1972." -- It is well known to the Soviet Union that the U.S... understanding of this phrase is that the Parties agreed that the ABM Treaty permits the testing of certain ABM systems in space. -- Recent statements by the Soviet side suggest that the exercise of these existing rights under the ABM Treaty would constitute a violation of this Treaty and grounds for withdrawal from a future START Treaty. -- This is simply not the case. The exercise of existing rights to test ABM systems based on other physical principles in space is not a violation of the ABM Treaty and, as such, does not constitute grounds for the Soviet Union to avoid its current or future Treaty obligations. -- Since our meeting in Wyoming, Soviet attempts to "relink" START with constraints on the SDI program have become an obstacle to progress in negotiations and, if not dropped, will obstruct ratification of a START agreement. -- The United States proceeds towards a START agreement with the understanding that Soviet compliance with a START agreement will not be conditioned on the requirement that the United States waive or forfeit rights under the ABM Treaty. SEEREP 1292 | CHRON FILE . THE WHITE HOUSE WASHINGTON February 21, 1990} E PRESIDENT Has SEEN MEETING WITH SENATORS WARNER AND NUNN .. DATE: February 22, 1990 DECLASSIFIED LOCATION: Oval Office PER E.O 13526 TIME: 1:30 pm - 2:00 pm Lell- 2806- MR 2la}ro1e ee FROM: BRENT SCOWCROFT I. PURPOSE To discuss Congressional support for the defense budget, especially the strategic modernization program and its relationship to START. II. BACKGROUND This is an informal meeting held at the request of Senators Nunn and Warner to express their concerns about the prospects on the Hill for the strategic modernization program. They will tell you that the "two-missile program" will not survive without a major arms control initiative and that B-2 and SDI are "in trouble." You will want to draw them out on these subjects as well as on Congressional support for troop levels in Europe and on the defense budget as a whole. Support for strategic modernization last year was very thin and required the investment of considerable political capital and time by you, Dick Cheney and our arms control negotiators. The political climate this year is far less supportive. We face a major attack on the defense budget, the belief by some that the strategic threat is now appreciably lessened, a growing clamor for payment of the "peace dividend," and election year politics. Yet with START in the balance, strategic modernization may be even more important. ICBM Modernization. Your FY 1991 defense budget calls for $2.8 billion for the Peacekeeper ICBM, most of which is for the redeployment of the missile from its silos to rail garrison. It also includes $202 million for continued development of the small mobile ICBM, the Midgetman. The first trains with Peacekeeper would be deployed in 1992 and the first Midgetman in 1997. The two missiles have survived in Congress as a package. The coalition that has supported both missiles was built largely around political compromises to support one missile in return for support for the other, and was never very cc: Vice President SECRET Chief of Staff Declassify on: OADR

seen 2 SEGREF 4 strong. That coalition is vanishing and there has not been a majority who support either missile for a year or more. Now Warner has said that we cannot afford both systems; Nunn has called for a revision to START to ban mobile MIRVs and said that we should delay the treaty if necessary to achieve this; Aspin has said the US can no longer justify systems as needed for bargaining leverage. These are the members who led the fight to support the two missile program last year. If both programs are cancelled there is a real possibility that the START treaty would not be ratified since the Soviets already have two deployed mobile systems and we will have none. This problem will be compounded if the B-2 program is sharply curtailed and we are not able to take full advantage of the bomber counting rule in START. Nunn will push for a change to our START position to explicitly ban mobile MIRVs. While I am attracted to this idea, I am concerned that anything we do now will delay the conclusion of START and potentially give the Soviets an opening to charge that we are intentionally delaying the treaty. I will have some specific ideas to discuss with you soon. At this point, however, I note that there are no quick or simple ways to change START to rebuild support on the Hill for our ICBM modernization program. B-2. Your budget proposal calls for $5.5 billion and the authorization to produce five additional aircraft. Last fall Congress reduced the plan from five aircraft to two and added a number of testing and reporting requirements. This year support for the aircraft is weakening further, and the aircraft cost makes this an easy target (although about half of the total program cost already has been spent). The full program is for a buy of 132 aircraft and that is what is presented in your budget. Dick Cheney has ordered a full review of the program by March, after which he will make his recommendations. Best guess now is that he will call for a total buy of 75-85 aircraft instead of the 132 now planned. Nunn and Warner should be generally supportive of the program but will be concerned about the costs -- both monetary and political. Both have supported the technology but will not support the full 132 aircraft buy. Nunn argues, I think correctly, that we have not done a good job defining the mission for this aircraft. We do need the B-2 if we are to enter the next century with a penetrating bomber. Numbers much below 75 do not make much sense to me from either a financial or operational point of view. Congressional support of the B-2 will be important, however, for ratification of START. As you recall, bombers that do "SEEREP SEG R E

"Secnet COON 4 not carry ALCMs only count as one warhead against the 6000 weapon limit of the treaty. Without being able to take advantage of this treaty feature, the JCS will have real difficulty supporting the treaty. Trident/D-5. The Trident submarine program is in good shape on the Hill. This next year's budget funds the production of the 18th boat along with long-lead items for the 19th and 20th. We anticipate that START will permit this many boats. Next year, however, we will need to decide if we want to begin funding the 21st submarine. SDI. The FY 1991 budget calls for $4.8 billion for SDI. This level of funding is required to support large-scale space-based experiments necessary to making the decision on SDI deployment during your first term. Budget support has been eroding -- last year's initial request was for $5.6 billion, later revised in your budget to $4.9 billion and eventually funded at $3.9 billion. As with other programs, the changes in the political environment are increasing calls to cut back or cancel this program. In the past both Nunn and Warner supported the program and Nunn feels he carried the Administration's water during last fall's Congressional budget action. Some on the Hill question the Administration's support of SDI because we acquiesced to last year's cut in the program. We anticipate a substantial cut again this year unless you go to the mat for it. Nunn and Warner will want to know whether you are prepared to make an all out push including a veto threat if necessary. Nunn would likely be content with a program that focused on long-term R&D, bypassing the near-term deployment option. Warner may believe we are spending too much on near-term technologies. Nevertheless, as you were told at Livermore, Brilliant Pebbles accounts for only about three percent of the SDI budget, despite its high profile. The overall balance is about 50-50 near term and long term. Troop levels in Europe. Soviet agreement to your 195,000 manpower initiative in Central Europe should have strengthened Congressional support for maintaining this level, but there will be calls to reduce further as the Soviets inevitably pull out and German unification proceeds. While you will want to hear Nunn and Warner's views on how Congress will treat our military presence in Europe, you should emphasize that their support is essential and that we cannot now go lower without severe damage to the Alliance, the future of Germany, and our relations with our strongest allies. Other Defense Budget issues. You may want to discuss briefly the overall level of the defense budget of $295 al SEGREF

. SBE SEGREF 4 billion. The interest in the "peace dividend" seems limited at present to the press and those in Congress who want to shift even more funds into social programs, but there is widespread consensus that the budget will be cut, perhaps substantially. You can point out that the real peace dividends are in both peace itself and the potential for democratically elected governments in Eastern Europe. Nunn knows that there has been a reduction in defense spending every year since 1985 -- to take a bigger cut now may force hard-to-reverse steps before we fully understand the new strategic environment. Nunn and Warner can give insight on how to handle Congressional calls for substantial additional cuts. III. PARTICIPANTS List at Tab B. IV. PRESS PLAN White House photographer only. Vv. SEQUENCE Informal discussions. Tab A Points to be Made awe ld Tab B Participants List Naw a Page lek 'SBEREP

THE WHITE HOUSE RON FILE WASHINGTON April 11, 1990 MEMORANDUM FOR THE HONORABLE JAMES A. BAKER, III The Secretary of State THE HONORABLE DICK CHENEY The Secretary of Defense GENERAL COLIN L. POWELL, USA The Chairman, Joint Chiefs of Staff SUBJECT: START Issues to be Addressed Before the May Foreign Ministers' Meeting (U) During the meeting last week with Soviet Foreign Minister Shevardnadze, the two sides agreed on a list of issues to be addressed in the negotiations in preparation for the May 16-19 Ministerial meeting. These issues range from major policy issues on which the U.S. does not have a fully fleshed-out position to technical issues for which the Negotiator has primary responsibility. (gf The attached lists sort these issues into three groups, based on the level of attention they require in Washington. First are the major policy questions that reflect either internal USG or U.S.- Soviet differences. I have asked Arnie Kanter's "Ungroup" to press ahead on these and have suggested tentative dates for us to meet on those that may require our personal attention. (st Second is a list of the issues that the Arms Control Policy Coordinating Committee should resolve. This list may grow as the Delegation requests additional guidance in the weeks ahead. Issues that cannot be resolved at the PCC will be quickly advanced for more senior-level consideration. This schedule is being circulated separately to PCC members. (8) Third is a list of those issues for which the Negotiator has instructions or is best positioned to recommend next steps. (SJ Certainly the progress we will be able to make toward an agreement depends critically on the Soviets. For our part, however, we must be ready to take full advantage of any opportunities that present themselves. "SEGREP SEGRETF 2722 NATIONAL SECURITY COUNCIL WASHINGTON, D.C. 20506 April 9, 1990 ACTION MEMORANDUM FOR BRENT SCOWCROFT THROUGH: JOHN GORDON, actinc A SIGNED FROM: RICH DAVIS\d= SUBJECT: START Issues For The May Ministerial The Baker-Shevardnadze meeting last week produced a list of U.S.- Soviet issues in START to be worked before the next Ministerial, May 16-19. The length of the list (Tab A) shows how much needs to be done between now and then, both here and in Geneva if we are to meet the President's goal for the June Summit. The attached memorandum (Tab I) from you to your counterparts is designed to organize the START issues in the weeks ahead by allocating each issue to the group best able to reach informed decisions rapidly. If we are to be prepared to take full advantage of any openings the Soviets might offer, we need to complete our homework in a number of areas. The major issues are included in the first list and we recommend that you schedule tentative meetings now among your counterparts to address these issues. Suggested dates are included on the list. The second and third lists allocate "secondary" issues to the PCC and the Negotiator. It would be useful to include these lists in the package to demonstrate the importance you place on all of this work. We have informally coordinated the work schedule with Reg Bartholomew. co Tee. ----___. Robert Blackwill and Philip Zelikow concur. RECOMMENDATION That you sign the attached memorandum to your counterparts. Attachment Tab I Memorandum on START Issues For The May Ministerial Tab A Lists of START Issues SEEREP DECLASSIFIED Declassify on: OADR PER E.0O. 13526 Lejo-rB0b-mMe InJz}roye We

~: UNCLASSIFIED RECORD ID: 9008410 : NSC/S PROFILE RECEIVED: 22 OCT 90 19 TO: PRESIDENT CHRON FILE FROM: UNRECORDED DOC DATE: 07 MAR 90 SOURCE REF: KEYWORDS: START ARMS CONTROL & PERSONS: SUBJECT: BUILDING ON START / PROPOSALS TO BE CONSIDERED BY PRES ACTION: NOTED BY PRES W/ COMMENT DUE DATE: 25 OCT 90 STATUS: C STAFF OFFICER: NONE LOGREF: FILES: PA NSCP: CODES: DOCUMENT DISTRIBUTION FOR ACTION FOR _CONCURRENCE FOR INFO NSC CHRON COMMENTS: DISPATCHED BY DATE BY HAND W/ATTCH OPENED BY: NSRLG CLOSED BY: NSRLG DOC 1 OF 1 UNCLASSIFIED

THE PAESIDENT HAS Bh | E | Pa) "ge ~SEERET- we w ) in we Set Ww" yy / sh Hes 2 APN ww yh XP BUILDING ON START 6) yw ° A Qe ----- There is now great momentum in every area Of arms control, 4 especially START. If we continue at the current pace, we will be able to initial the START Treaty at the June Summit. = President Bush wants to use the momentum we have generated \S and move boldly to build on our achievements. ay -- This means focusing not just on numbers but on measures that can truly enhance strategic stability. There are two specific ways to do this: aad The first is to eliminate mobile MIR == w, before YY both sides have so sted in these systems that their elimination will be very difficult. ~The second is to eliminate, over a longer period of time, the remaining land-based MIRVs -- namely those in silos. -- I will outline these concepts briefly and would like to discuss them in more detail at the Ministerial, including whether we can begin to make these stabilizing improvements in the current negotiations. But let me emphasize that we J do not want to do anything to delay initialing START at the Washington Summit. -- We think a ban on mobile MIRVs could be incorporated into J START without delay. The US is prepared to cancel the railgarrison system in exchange for the elimination of your mobile gS-24, I know you have several SS-24 trains ployed, but the US also has a developed system, a budget o deploy it rapidly, and the political ability to proceed if necessary. ad We are also prepared to eliminate silo-based MIRVs over a longer period of time, although we recognize that this proposal would require extensive negotiations. But if the two sides are able to go down this deMIRVing path, it would be possible to side-step remaining differences about constraints on heavy ICBMs in the current START negotiations. a= If you see merit in an approach along these lines, we should work the details at the Washington Ministerial at the end of which President Bush and President Gorbachev could announce an agreement to ban mobile MIRVs in START, to initial the -SEGREP mt) Aceves PER E.O. 13526 Lelo-280b-me I2Ja}rele Man

"SEEREE START agreement at the Washington Summit, and to immediately turn to the longer-term goal of eliminating land-based MIRVs. == On the other hand, if you cannot agree to a ban on mobile MIRVs in the current START agreement, perhaps the two sides could announce at the April Ministerial that we would begin talks, immediately after the current START agreement is initialed in June, to eliminate first mobile MIRVs and then all land-based MIRVs. ie I wanted to present these ideas to you now so that you will have time to consider them. I know you have ideas of your own for follow-on negotiations which we can discuss at our April meeting. -- In sum, we are prepared to agree to ban mobile MIRVed ICBMs in the current START agreement provided that can be done without delaying the Treaty, and to address other stabilizing measures in follow-on negotiations that could begin as soon as the Treaty is initialed. == If you cannot agree to dealing with MIRV mobiles in the current negotiations, we could agree to turn immediately to these issues once the current START agreement is initialed at the Summit. = I hope that you agree that conditions will never be better for both sides to capture and build upon the momentum we . have generated in the negotiations. SECREP
